_metadata.description: Wilson's Three Domains of Learning — Cognitive (Bloom 1956 / Anderson-Krathwohl 2001), Affective (Krathwohl 1964), Psychomotor (Harrow 1972). The academic-research framing sibling of taxonomy_explorer_knowledge (BYUI tool view). Adds horizontal axis (domain coverage) to vertical axis (Hattie phase progression).
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~10,539 chars / 4 ≈ 2,635 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~12 knowledge files per audit task. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits.

scope: Academic-research framing for the three learning domains (Wilson 2001), including the affective-domain-matters argument, the physical ≠ psychomotor boundary rule, and audit interpretation across all three domains. Boundaries: BYUI's institutional tool view of the same three domains lives in taxonomy_explorer_knowledge (uses Simpson 7-level psychomotor instead of Harrow's 6-level).

facts.definition: Hattie tells how learning progresses; the Three Domains tell what kinds of learning a course is designing for. A course can sequence cognitive learning beautifully and still miss the affective and psychomotor objectives its outcomes implicitly require. A learning objective only 'lives in' a domain if the course intentionally designs for growth in that domain — activity that happens to involve feeling or movement does not count unless the design names it.
citations: Wilson, L.O. (2001, updated). Three Domains of Learning.

facts.definition: The thinking taxonomy — knowledge, comprehension, reasoning. Bloom 1956 vs Anderson/Krathwohl 2001: the 2001 revision changed noun → verb and swapped the top two levels (Creating now sits above Evaluating). Levels: (1) Knowledge/Remembering, (2) Comprehension/Understanding, (3) Application/Applying, (4) Analysis/Analyzing, (5) Synthesis→Evaluating (swapped), (6) Evaluation→Creating (swapped, now highest). The newer version is preferred for new instructional planning.

Five levels of internalization with sample verbs: (1) Receiving — awareness, willingness to attend (feel, sense, capture, experience, attend, perceive); (2) Responding — active attention (conform, allow, cooperate, contribute, enjoy, satisfy); (3) Valuing — acceptance or commitment to a value (believe, seek, justify, respect, persuade); (4) Organization — sorting values into a coherent system (examine, clarify, systematize, integrate); (5) Characterization — values become a stable life pattern (internalize, resolve, judge).

facts.term: Why Affective Matters in Any Course
facts.definition: Wilson's argument applies even to 'non-emotional' subjects (math, sciences, IT): 'emotion draws both attention and channels strong residual memory… it behooves all dedicated and artful educators to include affective objectives, no matter what their discipline.' Affective objectives raise the memorability of cognitive content — directly relevant to long-term retention. Affective growth shows up in: group/cooperative work, intellectual risk-taking, persuading peers, controversial/value-laden topics, self-confidence development through challenge. If outcomes imply these, the course should NAME an affective objective for them.

Six levels of physical/kinesthetic skill: (1) Reflex movements — involuntary, present at birth or via maturation; (2) Fundamental movements — walking, running, pushing, pulling, manipulating; (3) Perceptual abilities — kinesthetic, visual, auditory, tactile coordination; (4) Physical abilities — endurance, flexibility, agility, strength, dexterity; (5) Skilled movements — for games, sports, dances, performances, arts; (6) Nondiscursive communication — expressive movement (posture, gesture, mime, ballet).

If a physical activity SUPPORTS a cognitive or affective function, label it physical/kinesthetic — NOT psychomotor. Psychomotor means there is a very clear educational intention for growth in the psychomotor/kinesthetic domain itself. Examples: looking through a microscope to identify cells → cognitive; coding lab implementing SQL pipeline → cognitive; practicing surgical suture for proficiency → psychomotor; learning dance steps for performance → psychomotor. For most BYUI IT/Data courses, psychomotor objectives are rare — most 'hands-on' activity is physical-supporting-cognitive.

Targeted physical movement has the potential to enhance memory and recall and can aid in accelerating long-term memory. Intentionally adding movement to enhance learning is often called embodied learning. Even when the primary domain is cognitive, intentionally including movement (gestures while reasoning, walking while reflecting, building artifacts with hands) can strengthen schema formation.

Wilson's design recommendation: 'Wise teachers combine domains so that lessons and learning are more holistic and multidimensional. Mixing domains and using more diversity in delivering lessons also helps students create more neural networks and pathways thus aiding their retention and recall.' A course that addresses only the cognitive domain leaves retention strength on the table.

Outcomes mention collaboration, ethics, judgment, professional behavior → affective objective is implied, check if it's named. Discussions require students to take a position, persuade, or risk being wrong → affective design intent (Valuing or higher). No discussion/peer activity → affective domain absent (flag). Group projects with no rubric criteria for collaboration itself (only the deliverable) → affective happening incidentally, not designed. Reflection prompts asking students to evaluate own engagement/growth/beliefs → Characterization-level affective design.
citations: This knowledge file's Affective Audit table

facts.definition: Course outcomes name a physical skill (lab technique, performance, build) as the goal → psychomotor objective intentional, fully audit. 'Hands-on' activity but assessed outcome is conceptual → physical-supporting-cognitive, tag as cognitive not psychomotor. Course is fully online with no required physical artifact → psychomotor likely absent and that's fine. Movement/embodiment intentionally used to aid cognitive learning → embodied learning, note as cognitive enhancement.
citations: This knowledge file's Psychomotor Audit table

facts.definition: Value enum: cognitive (issue affects thinking/knowledge objectives); affective (issue affects feeling/value/motivation objectives); psychomotor (issue affects intentional physical-skill objectives); multi (issue spans more than one domain). Pair with hattie_phase and cognitive_load_type for full diagnosis.
citations: This knowledge file's Domain Tags section

sources: Wilson, L.O. (2001, updated). Three Domains of Learning — Cognitive, Affective, Psychomotor. The Second Principle.
Bloom, B.S. (1956). Taxonomy of Educational Objectives.
Krathwohl, D.R. (1964). Taxonomy of Educational Objectives: Affective Domain.
Anderson, L.W. & Krathwohl, D.R. (2001). A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing.
Harrow, A.J. (1972). A Taxonomy of the Psychomotor Domain.
